title: Write a Policy Brief
description: Research and write a two-page policy brief on a current Australian issue — housing, climate, healthcare, or education. Present the problem, analyse options, and recommend a course of action with supporting evidence.
whyItMatters: Policy briefs are the core communication tool of government. Ministers, shadow ministers, and senior advisers read policy briefs to make decisions. If you cannot condense a complex issue into a clear, evidence-based recommendation in two pages, you cannot operate in politics.

steps.title: Choose your issue
steps.description: Select a current Australian policy issue you care about. Check that there is recent data and reporting available.
steps.tips: Start with something specific, not broad. 'Homelessness in Sydney' is better than 'housing'.

steps.title: Research the evidence
steps.description: Find at least five credible sources — ABS data, Productivity Commission reports, academic research, and quality journalism.
steps.tips: Avoid opinion pieces as primary sources. Use them to understand the debate but cite data.

steps.title: Define the problem
steps.description: Write a clear problem statement in 100 words or fewer. What is wrong, how big is the problem, and who is affected?
steps.tips: Quantify wherever possible. 'Over 120,000 Australians are homeless on any given night' is more powerful than 'many people are homeless'.

steps.title: Analyse options
steps.description: Present three policy options including the status quo. For each, assess costs, benefits, feasibility, and political risks.
steps.tips: Include at least one option you personally disagree with — this demonstrates analytical rigour.

steps.title: Make your recommendation
steps.description: Choose one option and explain why. Address the strongest objection to your recommendation.
steps.tips: A good recommendation acknowledges trade-offs rather than pretending the choice is obvious.

steps.title: Format and polish
steps.description: Format as a proper policy brief: executive summary, problem statement, options analysis, recommendation, references. Two pages maximum.
steps.tips: Have someone outside politics read it. If they can understand the argument, you have written it clearly enough.

deliverables: A two-page policy brief with executive summary, problem statement, options analysis, and recommendation
A one-page appendix with data sources and key statistics

rubric.description: Is the problem clearly defined, quantified, and scoped?
Are sources credible, current, and properly cited?
Are at least three options presented with honest cost-benefit assessment?
Is the recommendation clear, justified, and does it address the strongest counter-argument?
Is the brief well-written, jargon-free, and within the two-page limit?
